games.explanation: Luzardo is a different pitcher depending on which side of the box you're standing in, and Miami happens to be sending a lineup stacked with the side that hits him. Against righties he's an ordinary mid-rotation arm, walking nine percent of the guys he faces and giving up enough hard contact to keep things interesting, which is a problem when Stowers and Ramirez are the bats waiting on him. Paddack is no prize either, but a coin-flip game where one side is paying you +124 doesn't need the starter to be sharp, it just needs the lineup to do what the splits say it should. The market is pricing this like Luzardo's overall line is the real him, and it isn't when the opponent bats from the right side.

Getting a plus price on the better Elo team is the kind of thing the market does when the logo on the jersey is louder than the matchup underneath it. Dustin May has real walk problems and Ohtani exists, but Wrobleski is a back-end arm being asked to navigate a Cardinals lineup that punishes righties, and Iván Herrera in particular has been a different hitter against this side. The wind is blowing in, the park is playing neutral, and the projected margin is a single run in a game priced like a mismatch. At +130, you're getting paid to take the team the math actually prefers.

Clay Holmes is being priced like a frontline starter against a lineup that does exactly what gives him trouble, which is take walks and barrel mistakes. The Angels run Neto, Trout, and Moncada at the top of the order, all right-handed bats with real damage profiles against a sinker guy who's walking nearly 9% of righties and giving up loud contact when they do swing. Kochanowicz is the obvious hole in the pitch, but the sim still lands on a one-run game because Holmes keeps gifting free baserunners and the wind is blowing in to mute Soto. At +115 on a team the math makes a small favorite, you're getting paid to take the side that should be chalk.

The market has these teams a tier apart, but Griffin Canning is the kind of starter who quietly hands the underdog a pulse every time out. He walks too many hitters to be trusted as a road favorite, and the White Sox lefty-leaning order is exactly the profile that's hit him hardest, with a 51% hard-hit rate against southpaws telling the real story. Colson Montgomery has been the loudest bat in this lineup against right-handers, and Benintendi gets a fastball-heavy arm he can ambush. At +158 on a game the sims see decided by a third of a run, you're getting paid like it's a mismatch when it's a coin flip.

picks.explanation: The market is pricing this like the Yankees are going to roll, and the projected scoreline says it's a one-run scrap where a free half-run shouldn't be hanging around at even money. Fried is the kind of lefty whose surface numbers look cleaner than the underlying contact, and Baltimore's lineup is built to make him uncomfortable from the right side, with Henderson posting a .358 xwOBA against righties that translates fine here given Fried's vulnerability to hard contact. Yankee Stadium with the wind blowing out plays into a Baltimore offense that doesn't need much help to keep this within a run, and the simulated final lands at 5.2 to 4.8. Getting plus money to lose by one is the entire pitch, and the board is daring you to take it.
